title: Topic A: Assignment
text:
Instructions
This assignment asks you to reconstruct and evaluate arguments about whether Pastafarianism qualifies as a religion. Answers to Tasks 1–3 should be submitted as an Argdown file (.ad). Task 4 requires a short written commentary (max. 300 words) in PDF or plain text.

title: Topic A: Assignment
text:
Tasks

Task 1: Reconstruct an Argument
Reconstruct the following passage as a premise–conclusion argument in Argdown notation. Identify at least two premises and a conclusion, and give the argument a meaningful name.

“Courts should not grant religious exemptions to Pastafarians. Pastafarianism was explicitly designed as a parody by its founder. A belief system created as parody cannot be the object of sincere religious belief. And without sincere belief, there is no religion — and without religion, there can be no religious exemption.”



Task 2: Formulate an Objection
Which premise of the argument from Task 1 is most vulnerable to objection? Formulate a counter-argument in Argdown notation and connect it to the appropriate premise using an attack relation (-).


Task 3: Dialectical Map
Using argdown-map notation, produce a dialectical map that includes:

The argument from Task 1
Your counter-argument from Task 2
At least one additional statement node (supporting or being attacked)



Task 4 (optional): Case Study
Locate a real court ruling or administrative decision in which a Pastafarian claimed a religious exemption in any country. Briefly answer:

What legal right was claimed?
What did the court/authority decide?
Which premise of the argument from Task 1 does the court’s reasoning most directly support or undermine?

Cite your source using Quarto’s citation syntax: [@key] for a bibliography entry, or a full reference if the source is not in the course bibliography.

title: Topic A: Assignment
text:
Submission
Submit your Argdown file and the Task 4 commentary (if attempted) via the course platform by 2026-10-20. File naming: [matriculation-number]-topic-a.ad and [matriculation-number]-topic-a-commentary.pdf.

title: Introduction: Getting started

Learning Objectives

Understand the scope and goals of the course
Know the assessment format and grading criteria

title: Introduction: Getting started
text:
Content

What is Pastafarianism?
The Church of the Flying Spaghetti Monster (FSM) was founded in 2005 by Bobby Henderson, a physics graduate from Oregon State University. Henderson’s founding document was an open letter to the Kansas State Board of Education, which had recently voted to allow intelligent design alongside evolutionary theory in public school science classes. In the letter, Henderson demanded equal class time for his own supernatural creation theory: that the universe was created by an omnipotent deity made of spaghetti and meatballs (Cho 2023).
The letter went viral. In 2006, Henderson published The Gospel of the Flying Spaghetti Monster, which codified the movement’s doctrine. Followers are known as Pastafarians — a portmanteau of pasta and Rastafarianism.


Core Tenets







Tenet
Content




Creator
The FSM created the universe “while quite drunk,” touching the Earth with his noodly appendage


Scripture
The Gospel of the Flying Spaghetti Monster (Henderson, 2006)


History
The FSM deliberately hides evidence of creation to test the faith of believers


Holy figures
Pirates are the original Pastafarians; the decline of piracy causes global warming


Moral code
“The Eight I’d Really Rather You Didn’ts”


Afterlife
Heaven, which contains a beer volcano and a stripper factory


Ritual dress
A pasta colander worn on the head (used in official ID photos in several countries)





Origins and Context
Henderson’s protest had an immediate effect: the Kansas Board reversed its ruling in 2006. The open letter, however, took on a life of its own. What began as targeted political argumentation became a movement with genuine adherents across multiple countries.
The question of whether those adherents hold their beliefs sincerely — and what follows from that — has become a live legal and philosophical issue. Courts in the United States, Germany, Austria, New Zealand, the Netherlands, and Italy have all considered Pastafarian claims (Palmisano and Ranzato 2025).


Henderson’s Founding Argument
The logical structure of Henderson’s letter can be formalised as a premise–conclusion structure:
[Equal time]: The Flying Spaghetti Monster theory deserves equal time
              in public school science classes.

<Equality argument>

(1) If one supernatural creation theory receives time in public school science classes,
    all other supernatural theories are equally entitled to time.
(2) Intelligent design is a supernatural creation theory that received time
    in Kansas public school science classes.
----
(3) [Equal time]






Note



Note on premise (1): Henderson treats this as a conditional applying to any supernatural theory. A natural objection is that premise (1) only holds if the theories are sufficiently similar in evidential status. This objection — and others — will be reconstructed in Session 2.

title: Introduction: Getting started
text:
Exercises

Identify the premises. State the two premises of Henderson’s equality argument in your own words. Are both plausible? Explain briefly.
Challenge a premise. Which premise is easier to attack? Formulate a one-sentence objection.
Reconstruct in Argdown. Using the Argdown notation from the session, reconstruct the following objection as a counter-argument to [Equal time]:

“Intelligent design is not a scientific theory at all. It makes no testable predictions and therefore has no place in a science class, regardless of how many other theories do or do not.”

Research question. Find one example of a Pastafarian claiming a legal right (e.g. to wear a colander in an ID photo) in any country. Briefly describe the outcome.

title: Introduction: Getting started
section: What is the Flying Spaghetti Monster?
text:
What is the Flying Spaghetti Monster?

2005: Bobby Henderson writes an open letter to the Kansas State Board of Education
Proposes that the universe was created by a Flying Spaghetti Monster (FSM)
If intelligent design gets science-class time, the FSM deserves equal time
The letter goes viral — the Church of the Flying Spaghetti Monster is born
Followers: Pastafarians (pasta + Rastafarianism)







Note


“I and many others around the world are of your belief in a Creator. We just choose a different view of that creator.” — Bobby Henderson, 2005

Background:

Kansas Board of Education votes to allow intelligent design in science class alongside evolution
Reopens church–state separation debate in public education

Henderson’s move:

Open letter demanding equal class time for FSM creationism (Cho 2023)
Core claim: if one supernatural creation theory is in, all others must be too
Effect: the Kansas Board reversed its decision in 2006

title: Introduction: Getting started
text:
Why It Matters
Philosophy of religion:

What defines a religion? Belief? Ritual? Community? Cosmology?
Does a parodic origin disqualify a belief system from counting as a religion?

Law: (Martin 2020)

Courts in the US, Germany, Austria, New Zealand, and Italy have decided Pastafarian cases
Issues: military exemptions, oath ceremonies, ID photos with colander
Key question: must beliefs be sincere to deserve legal protection?

title: Introduction: Getting started
text:
Summary

Pastafarianism originated as satirical protest against intelligent design in Kansas (2005)
It has grown into a movement raising genuine questions about the definition and legal status of religion
The core question: does a satirical origin disqualify a belief system from being a religion?
Next session: reconstructing the “Is Pastafarianism a religion?” argument in detail





Cho, Hyun Jai (violet). 2023. “Tax Exemptions, Spaghetti, and the Devil: The Perils of Ambiguous Limitations on Religion and a Proposed Definition for Legally Acknowledged Systems of Faith.” Przegląd Konstytucyjny, no. 3: 61–82.


Martin, Nick. 2020. “Exemptions, Sincerity and Pastafarianism.” Journal of Applied Philosophy 37 (2): 258–72. https://doi.org/10.1111/japp.12386.

title: Is Pastafarianism a Religion?
text: The Sincere Belief Argument and the Parody Objection & Introduction to Argdown

title: Is Pastafarianism a Religion?
text:
Overview
This session examines the central question raised by Pastafarianism: is it a religion? We approach this not as a matter of personal opinion but as a logical and philosophical problem — and we use Argdown to make the argument structure explicit.
The question has legal stakes. Courts in multiple jurisdictions have had to decide whether Pastafarian claims deserve the same legal protection as those of established religions. Their reasoning turns on what counts as a religion and what makes religious belief sincere (Martin 2020).

The Three Arguments at a Glance
The session works through three connected arguments:
[FSM is a religion]: Pastafarianism qualifies as a religion for legal purposes.

<Sincere belief argument>
  + [FSM is a religion]

<Parody objection>
  - [FSM is a religion]

<Parody-sincerity response>
  - <Parody objection>
Key concepts: sincere belief, functional vs. substantive definitions of religion, satirical origin, burden of proof.

title: Introduction
text: Pastafarianism and the question of religion

title: Introduction
text:
Overview
In 2005, Bobby Henderson — a physics graduate from Oregon State University — sent an open letter to the Kansas State Board of Education. The Board had just voted to allow intelligent design alongside evolutionary theory in public school science classes. Henderson proposed that if one supernatural creation story got class time, so should another: the Flying Spaghetti Monster (FSM), a deity made of spaghetti and meatballs who created the universe “while quite drunk.”
The letter went viral. Henderson’s movement — Pastafarianism (a portmanteau of pasta and Rastafarianism) — grew into the Church of the Flying Spaghetti Monster. Its followers wear colanders as ritual headgear, revere pirates as holy figures, and attribute global warming to the decline of piracy.
For philosophers and legal scholars, Pastafarianism raises a serious question: if a belief system fulfils all standard criteria for religion — cosmology, scripture, ritual, community — does the satirical intent of its founder change anything?

Henderson’s Founding Argument
[Equal time]: FSM creationism deserves equal time in public school science class.

<Equality argument>

(1) If one supernatural creation theory receives time in public school science classes,
    all others deserve equal time.
(2) Intelligent design received time in Kansas public school science classes.
----
(3) [Equal time]
Key concepts for this session: satirical religion, intelligent design, church–state separation, argument reconstruction.

text:
What is Argdown? The Word Analogy









Text world
Argument map world




Format/Syntax
Docx format for texts
Argdown format for argument maps


Artefacts/Products
Written text
Argument map / dialectical structure


Tools to create/edit/export the artefacts
Word, LibreOffice
VS Code + Argdown Extension, Editor, Argdown Sandbox, Argdown CLI

title: Argdown
text:
Comparison of Argdown Tools

VSCode + Argdown Extension

Collaboration via Live Share Extension. (Also useful for live reconstructions and collaborative exercises with students.)

VS Code Web:

Extensions can be installed “permanently”. (https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/setup/vscode-web#_extensions)
Opening local folders: not supported in Firefox (but works in Chrome and Edge) (https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/setup/vscode-web#_file-system-api)

Argdown Sandbox: Runs directly in the browser.

Save and load only via copy & paste.

Argdown CLI

Command-line tool: export to many formats (besides image files: json, graphml, dot, html)

title: Argdown
text:
Graph Layout
👉 Argument maps use layout algorithms to position nodes and edges.
Options for manual layout adjustments:

Manual layout:

Export to graphml and edit in other programs (e.g. yEd).
Export to dot and use all customisation options of Graphviz.

Within Argdown:

Set Graphviz settings: https://argdown.org/guide/changing-the-graph-layout.html#layouting-with-viz-js

title: Argdown
text:
Representing Dialectical Structures
===
selection:
    excludeDisconnected: false
===

<Argument with intermediate conclusions — one node>

(1) First premise
(2) Second premise
----
(3) *Therefore:* Intermediate conclusion
(4) One more premise.
----
(5) *Therefore:* Conclusion.

 — one node




By default, disconnected nodes are not shown.1

title: Argdown
section: Dialectical Structures: Internal Structure of Individual Arguments1
text:
Dialectical Structures: Internal Structure of Individual Arguments1
===
selection:
  statementSelectionMode: all # adds all statements as nodes to the map
model:
  explodeArguments: true # adds argument nodes for each inferential step of each argument
map:
  argumentLabelMode: none # no text in argument nodes
  statementLabelMode: text # no titles in statement nodes
dot:
  argument:
    shape: circle # argument nodes are in the shape of circles
    minWidth: 0.2 # let's make those circles really small
  graphVizSettings:
    rankdir: TB # arrows flow from top to bottom (to produce a tree-like structure)
===

<Argument with intermediate conclusions — one node>

(1) First premise
(2) Second premise
----
(3) *Therefore:* Intermediate conclusion
(4) One more premise.
----
(5) *Therefore:* Conclusion.

title: Argdown
section: Inference Patterns & Logical Form: Simple Representation
text:
Inference Patterns & Logical Form: Simple Representation
(1) First premise
    *Form: If p, then q.*
(2) Second premise
    *Form: p*
-- from 1, 2, by Modus Ponens --
(3) *Therefore:* Conclusion





Argument Map

title: Argdown
section: Inference Patterns & Logical Form: Detailed Representation
text:
Inference Patterns & Logical Form: Detailed Representation
(1) First premise
    *Form: If p, then q.*
    p: _what p stands for_
    q: _what q stands for_
(2) Second premise
    *Form: p*
-- 
from 1, 2, by Modus Ponens
--
(3) *Therefore:* Conclusion





Argument Map

title: Argdown
section: Inference Patterns & Logical Form: Machine-Readable Representation (JSON/YAML)
text:
Inference Patterns & Logical Form: Machine-Readable Representation (JSON/YAML)
(1) First premise {formalization: "p -> q", declarations: {"p": "what p stands for", "q": "what q stands for"}}
(2) Second premise {formalization: "p"}
-- {from: ["1","2"], logic: ["modus ponens"]} --
(3) *Therefore:* Conclusion





Argument Map



👉 https://argdown.org/syntax/#statement-yaml-data

title: Topic A: Core concepts
text:
Learning Objectives

Explain the fundamental concepts of Topic A
Apply the basic methods to simple examples

title: Topic A: Core concepts
text:
Content

What Makes Something a Religion?
There is no universally agreed definition of “religion” in philosophy, sociology, or law. Two broad approaches dominate the debate:

Substantive definitions focus on what a religion is: belief in supernatural entities, sacred texts, and a community of adherents.
Functional definitions focus on what a religion does: providing ultimate meaning, community, a moral framework, and ways of coping with finitude.

Pastafarianism satisfies many criteria on both approaches. Whether that is sufficient — given its explicitly parodic origin — is the central question of this session.


The Sincere Belief Argument
Martin (2020) argues that the decisive criterion is sincerity: a belief system qualifies as a religion for legal purposes if its adherents sincerely hold its tenets and are prepared to bear real costs for those beliefs. On this view, the origin of the belief system (parodic or otherwise) is irrelevant; only the current attitude of the believer matters.
[FSM is a religion]: Pastafarianism qualifies as a religion for legal purposes.

<Sincere belief argument>

(1) A belief system constitutes a religion if its adherents sincerely hold its tenets
    and are prepared to bear costs for those beliefs.
(2) Some Pastafarians hold the tenets of Pastafarianism sincerely and have borne real
    costs (legal disputes, official complaints, social censure) for their identity.
----
(3) [FSM is a religion]


The Parody Objection
The common objection to Pastafarian claims is that the movement’s explicitly satirical origin disqualifies it:
<Parody objection>

(1) A belief system deliberately designed as parody cannot be the object of sincere belief.
(2) Pastafarianism was deliberately designed as parody by its founder.
----
(3) Pastafarianism cannot be sincerely held.
(4) A belief system that cannot be sincerely held does not qualify as a religion.
----
(5) Pastafarianism does not qualify as a religion.

[FSM is a religion]
  - <Parody objection>


The Response: Sincerity Can Outlast Satirical Origin
Martin (2020) argues in response that the parody objection conflates the founder’s intent with the adherents’ belief:
<Parody-sincerity response>

(1) A belief system may originate as satire yet be later adopted with genuine commitment
    by adherents who were not its founders.
(2) The sincerity criterion applies to current believers, not to the founder's intent.
(3) Some current Pastafarians satisfy a behavioural sincerity test: consistent practice
    and willingness to bear costs.
----
(4) The parodic origin of Pastafarianism does not establish that current adherents
    cannot hold it sincerely.

<Parody objection>
  - <Parody-sincerity response>


Full Dialectical Map
All three arguments in one view:





Argument Map


n0

FSM is a religion
Pastafarianism qualifies as a religion
 for legal purposes.



n1

Sincere belief argument



n1->n0





n2

Parody objection



n2->n0





n3

Parody-sincerity response



n3->n2

title: Topic A: Core concepts
text:
Exercises

Diagram the structure. Identify which argument supports [FSM is a religion], which attacks it, and which attacks an attacker. Check your reading against the dialectical map above.
Evaluate premise (1) of the parody objection. Is it plausible that a belief system designed as parody can never be sincerely held? Give a real-world example that either supports or undermines this premise.
Extend the map. Add a further counter-argument to [FSM is a religion] that does not rely on the satirical origin. Reconstruct it in Argdown notation and indicate where it fits in the dialectical map.
Compare definitions. Does the sincerity test count as a functional or a substantive definition of religion? Justify your answer in 3–5 sentences, citing Martin (2020).
